title: European Job Market Visualizer
intro1_html: This is a research tool that visualizes over 400 occupations from the ESCO framework, covering the European job market. Each rectangle's area is proportional to total employment. Color shows the selected metric — toggle between projected growth outlook, median pay, education requirements, and AI exposure. Click any tile to view its full ESCO page. This is not a report, a paper, or a serious economic publication — it is a development tool for exploring EU data visually.
intro2_html: LLM-powered coloring: The source code includes scrapers, parsers, and a pipeline for writing custom LLM prompts to score and color occupations by any criteria. You write a prompt, the LLM scores each occupation, and the treemap colors accordingly. The "Digital AI Exposure" option is one example — it estimates how much current AI (which is primarily digital) will reshape each occupation. But you could write a different prompt for any question — e.g. exposure to humanoid robotics, offshoring risk, climate impact — and re-run the pipeline to get a different coloring.
prompt_summary: View the Digital AI Exposure scoring prompt (example)
caveat_html: Caveat on Digital AI Exposure scores: These are rough LLM estimates, not rigorous predictions. A high score does not predict the job will disappear. Software developers score 9/10 because AI is transforming their work — but demand for software could easily grow as each developer becomes more productive. The score does not account for demand elasticity, latent demand, regulatory barriers, or social preferences for human workers. Many high-exposure jobs will be reshaped, not replaced.
methodology_html: Data Methodology: Total Employment reflects exact Eurostat census figures. Because granular 4-digit socio-economic data is statistically suppressed across all 27 member states, Growth, Pay, and Education metrics are algorithmic approximations imputed directly from official Eurostat 2-digit broad category averages.
quote_1: The market is efficient at finding the lowest possible pay for my specific suffering.
quote_2: AI won't replace you, but a human using AI to do three people's jobs for the price of one will.
quote_3: My degree is a very expensive bookmark for my student loan agreement.
quote_4: The 'Future of Work' looks suspiciously like the 'Presence of Burnout'.
quote_5: If hard work actually made you rich, my ancestors would have owned the planet.
quote_6: Automating my job was the best thing for the company. The worst were the layoffs.
quote_7: I'm currently in a committed relationship with my LinkedIn 'Open to Work' banner.
quote_8: The middle class is just a subscription service that we can no longer afford.
quote_9: Retirement is essentially a hypothetical scenario where I stop working before I die.
quote_10: Living the dream. Specifically, the one where I'm late for a meeting I don't remember.
quote_11: I used to have a career, now I have a gig that pays in 'exposure' and 'networking'.
quote_12: The robots won't kill us; they'll just make our skillsets obsolete every 18 months.
quote_13: We replaced the middle manager with a script. The script is less passive-aggressive.
quote_14: Success is 10% inspiration and 90% being born in the right decade.
quote_15: My AI assistant is great at summarizing the meetings I shouldn't have been in anyway.
